From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sensory perception of sound by living organisms

For other uses, see [Hearing (law)](/wiki/Hearing_(law) "Hearing (law)") and [Hearing (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hearing_(disambiguation) "Hearing (disambiguation)").

Several terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Hear (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hear_(disambiguation) "Hear (disambiguation)"), [Aural (disambiguation)](/wiki/Aural_(disambiguation) "Aural (disambiguation)"), and [Psychoacoustics](/wiki/Psychoacoustics "Psychoacoustics").

Video showing how sounds make their way from the source to the brain

**Hearing**, or **auditory perception**, is the ability to perceive [sounds](/wiki/Sound "Sound") through an organ, such as an [ear](/wiki/Ear "Ear"), by detecting [vibrations](/wiki/Vibration "Vibration") as periodic changes in the pressure of a surrounding medium.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The academic field concerned with hearing is [auditory science](/wiki/Auditory_science "Auditory science").

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Wikiversity_logo_2017.svg/40px-Wikiversity_logo_2017.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikiversity_logo_2017.svg)

Wikiversity has learning resources about ***[Global Audiology](https://en.wikiversity.org/wiki/Global_Audiology "v:Global Audiology")***

Sound may be heard through [solid](/wiki/Solid "Solid"), [liquid](/wiki/Liquid "Liquid"), or [gaseous](/wiki/Gas "Gas") matter.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is one of the traditional five [senses](/wiki/Sense "Sense"). Partial or total inability to hear is called [hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss").

In humans and other vertebrates, hearing is performed primarily by the [auditory system](/wiki/Auditory_system "Auditory system"): [mechanical waves](/wiki/Mechanical_wave "Mechanical wave"), known as vibrations, are detected by the [ear](/wiki/Ear "Ear") and [transduced](/wiki/Transduction_(physiology) "Transduction (physiology)") into nerve impulses that are perceived by the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") (primarily in the [temporal lobe](/wiki/Temporal_lobe "Temporal lobe")). Like [touch](/wiki/Touch "Touch"), audition requires sensitivity to the movement of molecules in the world outside the organism. Both hearing and touch are types of [mechanosensation](/wiki/Mechanosensation "Mechanosensation").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Peng_2011-4)

## Hearing mechanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Hearing mechanism")]

There are three main components of the human [auditory system](/wiki/Auditory_system "Auditory system"): the outer ear, the middle ear, and the inner ear.

### Outer ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Outer ear")]

Main article: [Outer ear](/wiki/Outer_ear "Outer ear")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Tidens_naturl%C3%A6re_fig40.png/250px-Tidens_naturl%C3%A6re_fig40.png)](/wiki/File:Tidens_naturl%C3%A6re_fig40.png)

Schematic diagram of the human ear[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The outer ear includes the [pinna](/wiki/Pinna_(anatomy) "Pinna (anatomy)"), the visible part of the ear, as well as the [ear canal](/wiki/Ear_canal "Ear canal"), which terminates at the [eardrum](/wiki/Eardrum "Eardrum"), also called the tympanic membrane. The pinna serves to focus sound waves through the ear canal toward the eardrum. Because of the asymmetrical character of the outer ear of most mammals, sound is [filtered](/wiki/Filter_(signal_processing) "Filter (signal processing)") differently on its way into the ear depending on the location of its origin. This gives these animals the ability to localize sound [vertically](/wiki/Vertical_sound_localization "Vertical sound localization"). The eardrum is an airtight membrane, and when sound waves arrive there, they cause it to vibrate following the [waveform](/wiki/Waveform "Waveform") of the sound. [Cerumen](/wiki/Cerumen "Cerumen") (ear wax) is produced by [ceruminous](/wiki/Ceruminous_gland "Ceruminous gland") and [sebaceous glands](/wiki/Sebaceous_gland "Sebaceous gland") in the skin of the human ear canal, protecting the ear canal and tympanic membrane from physical damage and microbial invasion.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Middle ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle ear")]

Main article: [Middle ear](/wiki/Middle_ear "Middle ear")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Gray919.png/250px-Gray919.png)](/wiki/File:Gray919.png)

The middle ear uses three tiny bones, the malleus, the incus, and the stapes, to convey vibrations from the eardrum to the inner ear.

The middle ear consists of a small air-filled chamber that is located medial to the eardrum. Within this chamber are the three smallest bones in the body, known collectively as the [ossicles](/wiki/Ossicles "Ossicles") which include the malleus, incus, and stapes (also known as the hammer, anvil, and stirrup, respectively). They aid in the transmission of the vibrations from the eardrum into the inner ear, the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea"). The purpose of the middle ear ossicles is to overcome the [impedance](/wiki/Acoustic_impedance "Acoustic impedance") mismatch between air waves and cochlear waves, by providing [impedance matching](/wiki/Impedance_matching "Impedance matching").

Also located in the middle ear are the [stapedius muscle](/wiki/Stapedius_muscle "Stapedius muscle") and [tensor tympani muscle](/wiki/Tensor_tympani_muscle "Tensor tympani muscle"), which protect the hearing mechanism through a stiffening reflex. The stapes transmits sound waves to the inner ear through the [oval window](/wiki/Oval_window "Oval window"), a flexible membrane separating the air-filled middle ear from the fluid-filled inner ear. The [round window](/wiki/Round_window "Round window"), another flexible membrane, allows for the smooth displacement of the inner ear fluid caused by the entering sound waves.

### Inner ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Inner ear")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Right_osseous_labyrinth_svg_hariadhi.svg/250px-Right_osseous_labyrinth_svg_hariadhi.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Right_osseous_labyrinth_svg_hariadhi.svg)

The inner ear is a small but very complex organ.

Main article: [Inner ear](/wiki/Inner_ear "Inner ear")

The inner ear consists of the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea"), which is a spiral-shaped, fluid-filled tube. It is divided lengthwise by the [organ of Corti](/wiki/Organ_of_Corti "Organ of Corti"), which is the main organ of [mechanical to neural transduction](/wiki/Neuronal_encoding_of_sound "Neuronal encoding of sound"). Inside the organ of Corti is the [basilar membrane](/wiki/Basilar_membrane "Basilar membrane"), a structure that vibrates when waves from the middle ear propagate through the cochlear fluid – [endolymph](/wiki/Endolymph "Endolymph"). The basilar membrane is [tonotopic](/wiki/Tonotopy "Tonotopy"), so that each frequency has a characteristic place of resonance along it. Characteristic frequencies are high at the basal entrance to the cochlea, and low at the apex. Basilar membrane motion causes [depolarization](/wiki/Depolarization "Depolarization") of the [hair cells](/wiki/Hair_cells "Hair cells"), specialized auditory receptors located within the organ of Corti.[[6]](#cite_note-6) While the hair cells do not produce [action potentials](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential") themselves, they release neurotransmitter at synapses with the fibers of the [auditory nerve](/wiki/Auditory_nerve "Auditory nerve"), which does produce action potentials. In this way, the patterns of oscillations on the basilar membrane are converted to [spatiotemporal patterns](/wiki/Spatiotemporal_pattern "Spatiotemporal pattern") of firings which transmit information about the sound to the [brainstem](/wiki/Brainstem "Brainstem").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Neuronal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Neuronal")]

Main article: [Neuronal encoding of sound](/wiki/Neuronal_encoding_of_sound "Neuronal encoding of sound")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Lateral_lemniscus.PNG/250px-Lateral_lemniscus.PNG)](/wiki/File:Lateral_lemniscus.PNG)

The [lateral lemnisci](/wiki/Lateral_lemnisci "Lateral lemnisci") (red) connects lower brainstem auditory nuclei to the [inferior colliculus](/wiki/Inferior_colliculus "Inferior colliculus") in the midbrain.

The sound information from the cochlea travels via the [auditory nerve](/wiki/Auditory_nerve "Auditory nerve") to the [cochlear nucleus](/wiki/Cochlear_nucleus "Cochlear nucleus") in the [brainstem](/wiki/Brainstem "Brainstem"). From there, the signals are projected to the [inferior colliculus](/wiki/Inferior_colliculus "Inferior colliculus") in the [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain") [tectum](/wiki/Tectum "Tectum"). The [inferior colliculus](/wiki/Inferior_colliculus "Inferior colliculus") integrates auditory input with limited input from other parts of the brain and is involved in subconscious reflexes such as the auditory [startle response](/wiki/Startle_response "Startle response").

The inferior colliculus in turn projects to the [medial geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Medial_geniculate_nucleus "Medial geniculate nucleus"), a part of the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") where sound information is relayed to the [primary auditory cortex](/wiki/Primary_auditory_cortex "Primary auditory cortex") in the [temporal lobe](/wiki/Temporal_lobe "Temporal lobe"). Sound is believed to first become consciously experienced at the [primary auditory cortex](/wiki/Primary_auditory_cortex "Primary auditory cortex"). Around the [primary auditory cortex](/wiki/Primary_auditory_cortex "Primary auditory cortex") lies [Wernicke's area](/wiki/Wernicke%27s_area "Wernicke's area"), a cortical area involved in interpreting sounds that is necessary to understand spoken words.

Disturbances (such as [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") or [trauma](/wiki/Trauma_(medicine) "Trauma (medicine)")) at any of these levels can cause hearing problems, especially if the disturbance is [bilateral](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bilateral "wikt:bilateral"). In some instances it can also lead to [auditory hallucinations](/wiki/Auditory_hallucinations "Auditory hallucinations") or more complex difficulties in perceiving sound.

## Hearing tests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hearing tests")]

Main articles: [Hearing test](/wiki/Hearing_test "Hearing test") and [Audiometry](/wiki/Audiometry "Audiometry")

Hearing can be measured by behavioral tests using an [audiometer](/wiki/Audiometer "Audiometer"). Electrophysiological tests of hearing can provide accurate measurements of hearing thresholds even in unconscious subjects. Such tests include [auditory brainstem evoked potentials](/wiki/Auditory_brainstem_response "Auditory brainstem response") (ABR), [otoacoustic emissions](/wiki/Otoacoustic_emissions "Otoacoustic emissions") (OAE) and [electrocochleography](/wiki/Electrocochleography "Electrocochleography") (ECochG). Technical advances in these tests have allowed hearing screening for infants to become widespread.

Hearing can be measured by mobile applications which includes audiological hearing test function or [hearing aid application](/w/index.php?title=Hearing_aid_application&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hearing aid application (page does not exist)"). These applications allow the user to measure [hearing thresholds](/wiki/Hearing_threshold "Hearing threshold") at different frequencies ([audiogram](/wiki/Audiogram "Audiogram")). Despite possible errors in measurements, [hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss") can be detected.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Hearing loss

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hearing loss")]

Main article: [Hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss")

There are several different types of hearing loss: [conductive hearing loss](/wiki/Conductive_hearing_loss "Conductive hearing loss"), [sensorineural hearing loss](/wiki/Sensorineural_hearing_loss "Sensorineural hearing loss") and mixed types. Recently, the term of [Aural Diversity](/wiki/Aural_diversity "Aural diversity") has come into greater use, to communicate hearing loss and differences in a less negatively-associated term.

There are defined degrees of hearing loss:[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

* **Mild hearing loss** - People with mild hearing loss have difficulties keeping up with conversations, especially in noisy surroundings. The most quiet sounds that people with mild hearing loss can hear with their better ear are between 25 and 40 dB HL.
* **Moderate hearing loss** - People with moderate hearing loss have difficulty keeping up with conversations when they are not using a hearing aid. On average, the most quiet sounds heard by people with moderate hearing loss with their better ear are between 40 and 70 dB HL.
* **Severe hearing loss** - People with severe hearing loss depend on powerful hearing aid. However, they often rely on lip-reading even when they are using hearing aids. The most quiet sounds heard by people with severe hearing loss with their better ear are between 70 and 95 dB HL.
* **Profound hearing loss** - People with profound hearing loss are very hard of hearing and they mostly rely on lip-reading and sign language. The most quiet sounds heard by people with profound hearing loss with their better ear are from 95 dB HL or more.

### Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Causes")]

* Heredity
* Congenital conditions
* Presbycusis
* Acquired
  + Noise-induced hearing loss
  + Ototoxic drugs and chemicals
  + Infection

### Prevention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Prevention")]

Hearing protection is the use of devices designed to prevent [noise-induced hearing loss](/wiki/Noise-induced_hearing_loss "Noise-induced hearing loss") (NIHL), a type of [post-lingual hearing impairment](/wiki/Post-lingual_hearing_impairment "Post-lingual hearing impairment"). The various means used to prevent hearing loss generally focus on reducing the levels of noise to which people are exposed. One way this is done is through environmental modifications such as [acoustic quieting](/wiki/Acoustic_quieting "Acoustic quieting"), which may be achieved with as basic a measure as lining a room with [curtains](/wiki/Curtain "Curtain"), or as complex a measure as employing an [anechoic chamber](/wiki/Anechoic_chamber "Anechoic chamber"), which absorbs nearly all sound. Another means is the use of devices such as [earplugs](/wiki/Earplug "Earplug"), which are inserted into the ear canal to block noise, or [earmuffs](/wiki/Earmuffs "Earmuffs"), objects designed to cover a person's ears entirely.

### Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Management")]

Main article: [Management of hearing loss](/wiki/Management_of_hearing_loss "Management of hearing loss")

The loss of hearing, when it is caused by neural loss, cannot presently be cured. Instead, its effects can be mitigated by the use of audioprosthetic devices, i.e. hearing assistive devices such as [hearing aids](/wiki/Hearing_aid "Hearing aid") and [cochlear implants](/wiki/Cochlear_implants "Cochlear implants"). In a clinical setting, this management is offered by [otologists](/wiki/Otology "Otology") and [audiologists](/wiki/Audiology#Audiologist "Audiology").

### Relation to health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Relation to health")]

[Hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss") is associated with [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease") and [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") with a greater degree of [hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss") tied to a higher risk.[[12]](#cite_note-12) There is also an association between [type 2 diabetes](/wiki/Type_2_diabetes "Type 2 diabetes") and [hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Hearing underwater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hearing underwater")]

Hearing threshold and the ability to [localize sound sources](/wiki/Sound_localization "Sound localization") are reduced underwater in humans, but not in aquatic animals, including whales, seals, and fish which have ears adapted to process water-borne sound.[[14]](#cite_note-DOSITS-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## In vertebrates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: In vertebrates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Domestic_cat_felis_catus.jpg/250px-Domestic_cat_felis_catus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Domestic_cat_felis_catus.jpg)

A cat can hear high-frequency sounds up to two octaves higher than a human.

Not all sounds are normally audible to all animals. Each species has a range of normal hearing for both amplitude and [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency"). Many animals use sound to communicate with each other, and hearing in these species is particularly important for survival and reproduction. In species that use sound as a primary means of communication, hearing is typically most acute for the range of pitches produced in calls and speech.

### Frequency range

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Frequency range")]

See also: [Hearing range](/wiki/Hearing_range "Hearing range")

Frequencies capable of being heard by humans are called [audio](/wiki/Audio_frequency "Audio frequency") or sonic. The range is typically considered to be between 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Frequencies higher than audio are referred to as [ultrasonic](/wiki/Ultrasound "Ultrasound"), while frequencies below audio are referred to as [infrasonic](/wiki/Infrasound "Infrasound"). Some [bats](/wiki/Microbat "Microbat") use ultrasound for [echolocation](/wiki/Animal_echolocation "Animal echolocation") while in flight. [Dogs](/wiki/Dog "Dog") are able to hear ultrasound, which is the principle of 'silent' [dog whistles](/wiki/Dog_whistle "Dog whistle"). [Snakes](/wiki/Snake "Snake") sense infrasound through their jaws, and baleen [whales](/wiki/Whale "Whale"), [giraffes](/wiki/Giraffe "Giraffe"), [dolphins](/wiki/Dolphin "Dolphin") and [elephants](/wiki/Elephant "Elephant") use it for communication. Some [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish") have the ability to hear more sensitively due to a well-developed, bony connection between the ear and their swim bladder. This "aid to the deaf" for fishes appears in some species such as [carp](/wiki/Carp "Carp") and [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring").[[17]](#cite_note-Williams_1941-17)

### Time discrimination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Time discrimination")]

Human perception of audio signal time separation has been measured to less than 10 microseconds (10μs). This does not mean that frequencies above 100 kHz are audible, but that time discrimination is not directly coupled with frequency range. Georg Von Békésy in 1929 identifying sound source directions suggested humans can resolve timing differences of 10μs or less. In 1976 Jan Nordmark's research indicated inter-aural resolution better than 2μs.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Milind Kuncher's 2007 research resolved time misalignment to under 10μs.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### In birds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: In birds")]

This section is an excerpt from [Bird anatomy § Hearing](/wiki/Bird_anatomy#Hearing "Bird anatomy").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bird_anatomy&action=edit)]

The avian ear is adapted to pick up on slight and rapid changes of pitch found in [bird song](/wiki/Bird_song "Bird song"). General avian [tympanic membrane](/wiki/Tympanic_membrane "Tympanic membrane") form is ovular and slightly conical. Morphological differences in the [middle ear](/wiki/Middle_ear "Middle ear") are observed between species. Ossicles within [green finches](/wiki/Greenfinch "Greenfinch"), [blackbirds](/wiki/Blackbird_(disambiguation) "Blackbird (disambiguation)"), [song thrushes](/wiki/Song_thrush "Song thrush"), and [house sparrows](/wiki/House_sparrows "House sparrows") are proportionately shorter to those found in [pheasants](/wiki/Pheasants "Pheasants"), [Mallard ducks](/wiki/Mallard_ducks "Mallard ducks"), and [sea birds](/wiki/Sea_birds "Sea birds"). In song birds, a [syrinx](/wiki/Syrinx_(bird_anatomy) "Syrinx (bird anatomy)") allows the respective possessors to create intricate melodies and tones. The middle avian ear is made up of three semicircular canals, each ending in an [ampulla](/wiki/Osseous_ampulla "Osseous ampulla") and joining to connect with the [macula sacculus](/wiki/Macula_sacculus "Macula sacculus") and [lagena](/wiki/Lagena_(anatomy) "Lagena (anatomy)"), of which the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea"), a straight short tube to the external ear, branches from.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## In invertebrates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hearing&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: In invertebrates")]

Even though they do not have ears, invertebrates have developed other structures and systems to decode vibrations traveling through the air, or “sound”. [Charles Henry Turner](/wiki/Charles_Henry_Turner_(zoologist) "Charles Henry Turner (zoologist)") was the first scientist to formally show this phenomenon through rigorously controlled experiments in ants.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Turner ruled out the detection of ground vibration and suggested that other insects likely have auditory systems as well.

Many insects detect sound through the way air vibrations deflect hairs along their body. Some insects have even developed specialized hairs tuned to detecting particular frequencies, such as certain caterpillar species that have evolved hair with properties such that it resonates most with the sound of buzzing wasps, thus warning them of the presence of natural enemies.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Some insects possess a [tympanal organ](/wiki/Tympanal_organ "Tympanal organ"). These are "eardrums", that cover air filled chambers on the legs. Similar to the hearing process with vertebrates, the eardrums react to sonar waves. Receptors that are placed on the inside translate the oscillation into electric signals and send them to the brain. Several groups of flying insects that are preyed upon by [echolocating](/wiki/Animal_echolocation "Animal echolocation") [bats](/wiki/Bat "Bat") can perceive the ultrasound emissions this way and reflexively practice [ultrasound avoidance](/wiki/Ultrasound_avoidance "Ultrasound avoidance").
